channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. and Iran Sign Preliminary Deal, but Its Terms Remain Secret
item.description: President Trump and Iran’s lead negotiator have signed an agreement that was expected to open the Strait of Hormuz. But the deal’s specific terms have not been released, and the countries have pushed their biggest disagreements to later talks.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: After Russia Attacks Kyiv With Missiles and Drones, Historic Orthodox Cathedral Burns
item.description: A bishop reported that many holy items had been recovered from the church, at the site of the Perchersk monastery, a revered place for the Ukrainian and Russian Orthodox faiths.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:description: A fire at the Dormition Cathedral in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Monday after a Russian attack.

item.title: UK Announces Social Media Ban for Children Under 16
item.description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer said his government planned to bar children under 16 from social media, following similar efforts in Australia and elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: High school students in Wimbledon, London, this year during an interview about social media. Britain plans to place an age limit on social media.

item.title: Inside Colombia’s Never-Ending Drug War
item.description: Ten years after a landmark peace deal, armed groups are wreaking deadly violence, blending jungle combat with new drone warfare.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Federico Rios
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: FARC guerrillas in Norte de Santander, Colombia, where they are clashing with another armed group, the ELN.

item.title: With Its Biggest E.U. Opponent Gone, Ukraine Is Advancing in Its Bid to Join
item.description: Even though negotiations will begin for Ukraine to join the bloc, the path ahead is a long one.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: A commemorative display in Mykolaiv, Ukraine, in March, honoring soldiers who have died in the war with Russia.

item.title: How Quebec Left the Church and Became the World Leader in Assisted Dying
item.description: A society once strongly Roman Catholic has rejected the church’s prohibition on euthanasia, with many instead viewing control over one’s death as an individual right.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi and Hubert Hayaud
item.media:description: In Lanaudière, 13 of 100 people end their lives through medically assisted dying.

item.title: U.K. Ban on Pro-Palestinian Protest Group Is Lawful, Court of Appeal Rules
item.description: The decision overturned a lower-court ruling that had found that banning Palestine Action as a terrorist group had breached free speech rights.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered on Monday as the Court of Appeal ruled on the government’s ban of Palestine Action.

item.title: What to Know About Planned Social Media Bans in Australia, the U.K. and Beyond
item.description: Britain said it would ban social media access for children under 16 starting in 2027, joining several other countries introducing similar measures.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: Students in Wimbledon, London, during an interview this year about social media. Public opinion in many countries has coalesced around the idea that more must be done to protect children online.

item.title: Son of Norway’s Crown Princess Is Convicted of Rape
item.description: Marius Borg Hoiby was sentenced to four years in prison. He stood trial as his mother came under pressure over her ties to Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Henrik Pryser Libell
item.media:credit: Lise Aserud/NTB Scanpix, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Marius Borg Hoiby of Norway, left, in Oslo in 2022.

item.title: How Ukraine Uses A.I. to Knock Deadly Russian Drones Out of the Skies
item.description: Interceptors show Ukraine’s embrace of autonomous technologies trained on immense troves of wartime data.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova

item.title: Many Iranians Express Relief Over Agreement to End the War
item.description: After enduring months of conflict, ordinary people in Iran were relieved to hear about the deal. Opposition groups were disappointed.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Demonstrators in Tehran on Sunday.

item.title: Switzerland Rejects Measure to Cap Its Population at 10 Million
item.description: The referendum was about limiting migration after the number of residents rose by more than a quarter since 2000, but it was framed around affordability and sustainability.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: A citizen casting her ballot on Sunday at a polling station in Bern, Switzerland, amid a vote on a divisive anti-immigration proposal to cap the country’s population and another referendum on restricting access to conscientious objection to military service.

item.title: UK Forces Seize Russian Shadow Fleet Oil Tanker
item.description: Britain’s Defense Ministry said it was the first time that British forces had acted alone to stop a ship in the fleet, a collection of vessels that Russia uses to move fuel and evade sanctions.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle

item.title: The G7 Summit Is Dogged by Chaos and Divided by Trump
item.description: Group of 7 meetings once embodied the effort to sustain the global diplomatic order. This year’s gathering, starting on Monday, symbolizes its fragmentation.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Leaders of the Group of 7 nations at a summit in Kananaskis, Canada, last year.

item.title: London Man Suspected in 2017 Shoving of a Woman Into Bus Path Is Arrested
item.description: The case had remained unresolved since May 2017, when a woman was sent tumbling into a bus lane on Putney Bridge. She escaped uninjured after the bus driver swerved.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: An image from a surveillance camera video released by the police shows the jogger who was sought by the police.

item.title: Two Men Found Guilty in Arson Attacks Targeting U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer
item.description: The men were convicted of conspiring to set fire to properties and a car linked to the prime minister after one of them was recruited on a Russian-language Telegram account.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain at Downing Street on Monday.

item.title: Jaded and Exhausted, Many Iranians Shrug at World Cup
item.description: The successes of Iran’s national soccer team used to be a source of celebration. Now, many Iranians can barely muster the energy to care.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Shirin Hakim and Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s national soccer team training in Carson, Calif., on Sunday. Many Iranians say their problems are too big these days for them to care much about how the team does.

item.title: In France, Justice System Faces Public Anger After 11-Year-Old Girl’s Killing
item.description: Protests have erupted across France after authorities revealed that a man accused of kidnapping Lyhanna Rameau Bernard, a French schoolgirl found dead this month, had previously been flagged to law enforcement.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: A silent march in tribute to Lyhanna Rameau Bernard, in Fleurance, France, this month.

item.title: Gulf Countries Confront Questions About Relying on U.S. for Protection
item.description: Iran targeted Bahrain, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and other American allies in the Middle East during the war, harming their economies and military sectors.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The sun setting in Kuwait last week. Iran has attacked the oil-rich country and other U.S. allies in the Middle East during the war.

item.title: Why the U.S.-Iran Deal Might Work, and Why It Might Not
item.description: For all of the cautious optimism surrounding the framework agreement, some of the toughest issues lie ahead, and the deal could still fall apart.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: The preliminary deal between Iran and the U.S. could still come apart.

item.title: Trump Winds Down the War He Started With Goals Unmet
item.description: While the president says the agreement with Iran would open the Strait of Hormuz and provide economic relief, the country’s nuclear program is still a subject for negotiation.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said on social media that the deal would “let the oil flow.”

item.title: Israel Counts the Ways That Netanyahu’s Iran Strategy Failed
item.description: The U.S.-Iran framework agreement appears to omit some of the most important provisions that Israel wanted.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel speaking in Jerusalem in April.

item.title: U.S.-Iran Agreement Leaves Lebanon’s Fate Murky
item.description: Iran claimed the deal would extend to fighting in Lebanon but no details of the agreement were made public and Israel said its forces would remain there.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Emergency response workers and security personnel at the site of an Israeli airstrike, in southern Beirut, Lebanon, on Sunday.

item.title: Deal to Reopen Hormuz Kicks Off Long Effort to Ease Energy Crisis
item.description: The pace of the recovery will depend on how confident companies are that the deal between the United States and Iran will hold and be extended.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz this month.

item.title: The U.S.-Iran Deal: What to Know
item.description: The full text of the deal that could pave the way to ending the war has not been published. Initial details suggest that it defers the most contentious issues.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Women in Tehran passing a mural showing Iranian missiles attacking an American ship.

item.title: China Moves the Price of Oil, Even When It Buys Less
item.description: China is the world’s largest purchaser of oil. But three months after the war with Iran began, it cut its imports, cushioning the global market.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Alexandra Stevenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Nanjing in China’s Jiangsu Province.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Find Ways to Claim Victory on the Nuclear Issue
item.description: President Trump said Iran had promised to suspend enriching uranium, but it is unclear for how long.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Natanz nuclear complex in Iran, in March in an image provided by Vantor, a satellite imaging company.

item.title: Taking on Neo-Nazis, One Trademark at a Time
item.description: In Germany, a campaign called Rights Against the Right is securing trademarks for right-wing symbols and phrases, aiming to cut into a revenue stream that sustains hate groups.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Schore Mehrdju for The New York Times
item.media:description: Among the latest trademarks awarded to Rights Against was for the term “ess ess,” code for S.S., or Schutzstaffel, the elite guard of the Nazi Reich.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Erica L. Green, Max Bearak, Lara Jakes and Sanam Mahoozi

item.title: World leaders welcome news that the Strait of Hormuz could soon reopen.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni

item.title: U.S. and Iran Reach Agreement to Reopen Strait and Begin Nuclear Talks
item.description: The accord halts hostilities in the war that began in February but leaves unresolved the critical issue of Iran’s nuclear program.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan, Farnaz Fassihi and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gathering in Tehran earlier this month., Iran affirmed in the agreement that it would never seek to build or procure a nuclear weapon, but the regime has made that promise many times previously.

item.title: Singer Oliver Tree Is Said to Have Died in Collision of Helicopters in Brazil
item.description: The two helicopters crashed in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday, killing at least six people, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:description: The helicopters crashed over a car dealership in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Claims Strait Will Be ‘Permanently Toll-Free’ Under Agreement With Iran
item.description: In a call to The New York Times, President Trump praised Russia’s and China’s leaders and described Israel’s prime minister as “a very difficult guy.”
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump insisted on Sunday that if Iran failed to reach a final nuclear accord with the United States, he would restart military attacks on Tehran.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Agree to a Truce
item.description: The agreement could pave the way for further talks that could ultimately end the war. President Trump said that it could also reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: In Israel, Broad Discontent Even Before Deal’s Details Are Known
item.description: Israelis across the political spectrum have said the agreement appears to leave fundamental security threats posed by Iran unaddressed.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in March. At the start of the recent war, he had said that its objective was “to remove the existential threats” to Israel.

item.title: Israel Strikes Beirut Outskirts as Fighting With Hezbollah Escalates
item.description: The attacks complicated an already delicate moment as President Trump and Iran appeared to be edging toward finalizing a framework peace agreement.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner and Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The site of an Israeli strike in Beirut’s southern outskirts on Sunday. Lebanese health authorities said three people were killed and 16 injured.

item.title: Net Gain
item.description: We’re covering the Knicks, and a savvy strategy people are using to game prediction markets.
item.dc:creator: Evan Gorelick
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: A watch party outside Madison Square Garden last night.

item.title: The Secrets of a Soccer-Turf Master
item.description: John Sorochan, a turf scientist at the University of Tennessee, has led the yearslong, multimillion-dollar effort to develop perfect playing fields for the 2026 World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: Deadlocked Wars: How Major Powers Misread the Regions They Attacked
item.description: Russia and the United States projected their own centralized views onto Ukraine and Iran, analysts said. As a result, the smaller countries trapped larger ones in a costly confrontation.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Early this year on the outskirts of Kostiantynivka, Ukraine, which has been bombed consistently.

item.title: Curaçao Is the Smallest Country Ever to Reach the World Cup
item.description: Competing for the first time, Curaçao is brimming with joy. Most of the Caribbean nation’s team was born and raised in the Netherlands, but residents say the players represent them.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Luis Antonio Rojas
item.media:description: Soccer players and artists at a celebration this month in Curaçao, a Caribbean island and constituent country of the Kingdom of the Netherlands.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi, Adam Rasgon, Ronen Bergman and Jonathan Swan

item.title: Japan Is Running Out of Royals. Are More Men the Answer?
item.description: Japan’s legislature is drafting a plan to allow the imperial family to adopt distant male relatives. But some in Japan would prefer a female emperor.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Emperor Naruhito of Japan, center, with Empress Masako and other royal family members at a garden party in April in Tokyo.

item.title: Germany and Japan Are Rearming Again, 80 Years After World War II
item.description: After becoming allies to disastrous effect in the 1940s, Berlin and Tokyo are finding new reasons to team up — including rebuilding their militaries.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Javier C. Hernández
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Shinjiro Koizumi, Japan’s defense minister, and his German counterpart, Boris Pistorius, at a naval base in Yokosuka, Japan, in March. The countries have been building up their militaries.

item.title: In Venezuela, a 91-Year-Old Weaver Sticks to Traditions
item.description: Though electric machines are now standard, the Venezuelan weaver Margarita Mora has clung to a mix of ancestral Indigenous and Spanish practices to create surprisingly modern work.
item.dc:creator: Silvia Benedetti and Andrea Hernández
item.media:credit: Andrea Hernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Top Haitian Security Official Kidnapped
item.description: A security expert who had recently become chief of staff to the new defense minister was abducted, the latest example of violence gripping the country.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: Cars burned by armed gangs in downtown Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in January.

item.title: 5 Children Are Killed After Van and S.U.V. Collide in Rural Ontario
item.description: The children — four girls and one boy — were among 10 people in the van at the time of the crash. An infant was seriously injured, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan and Abdi Latif Dahir

item.title: Unfinished Business
item.description: We like it when projects are completed, but what if we could abide comfortably in the toiling and striving it takes to get them there?
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch
item.category: Basilica i Temple Expiatori de la Sagrada Familia (Barcelona, Spain)

item.title: A Tren de Aragua Leader Is Killed in a Joint Strike, U.S. and Venezuela Say
item.description: A strike this week in Venezuela killed a gang leader known as Niño Guerrero who was wanted in the United States, officials in both countries said.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said the operation had been conducted in close consultation with the new Venezuelan government.

item.title: An Honored Canadian Journalist on the Importance of Investigative Reporting
item.description: Our Times colleague Susanne Craig was back home in Canada to be honored for her extraordinary career, which began in Calgary, Alberta, her hometown.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.category: Lucky Loser: How Donald Trump Squandered His Father's Fortune and Created the Illusion of Success (Book)

item.title: Brazilians Revive a World Cup Ritual to Cheer on Their National Team
item.description: After years of dampened enthusiasm about the World Cup, Brazilians are excited about their national team again — and they’re showing it by painting the streets.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Dado Galdieri
item.media:credit: Dado Galdieri for The New York Times

item.title: The Scientific Quest for Perfect World Cup Pitch
item.description: Every match must be played on natural grass that gives players as consistent a surface as possible, no matter the venue. Cue the years of sod studies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: With a Deal Seemingly Close, the U.S. Faces an Iran More Willing to Withstand Pressure
item.description: Iran’s new, more militaristic leaders have already survived the worst that America and Israel can deliver, and seem readier to take risks.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps in April at a government-organized march in Tehran.

item.title: Ebola Comes for Congo’s Most Vulnerable Children
item.description: The arrival of a sick newborn at Saint Nicholas Orphanage in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo set off an Ebola outbreak that quickly spread among the country’s most vulnerable. Local health authorities are now monitoring the children’s home, but at least two babies have already died.
item.dc:creator: Bethlehem Feleke, Michael Anthony Adams, Yasu Tsuji and Jon Hazell

item.title: In Latest Attacks, Russia Is Exploiting a Major Weakness for Ukraine
item.description: Ukraine is running out of American-made Patriot air-defense interceptors, and is pleading for more.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish, Kim Barker, Siobhán O’Grady and Lara Jakes
item.media:description: Ukrainian service members next to a launcher of a Patriot air-defense system, in an undisclosed location, in 2024.

item.title: At the Canadian Screen Awards, the Industry Steps Outside Hollywood’s Shadow
item.description: No longer content with being just “Hollywood North,” the Canadian screen industry is asserting itself and telling its own stories.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Cole Burston
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actor Robbie Graham-Kunz speaking with fans outside the 14th Canadian Screen Awards in Toronto.

item.title: Should Switzerland Cap Its Population at 10 Million? Voters Will Decide.
item.description: One of the world’s richest countries is about to hold a referendum on a measure that would curb migration and most likely the economy. It is being sold in warm tones.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: Campaign posters ahead of the population cap vote. President Trump’s face is on a no poster, with the slogan, “Now, of all times, a break with Europe?” The yes slogan shown is “Protect Switzerland.”

item.title: A Family Ravaged by Land Mines in Myanmar
item.description: Bu Ri lost a leg to a mine in Myanmar decades ago. More recently, six other members of his family have suffered a similar fate or worse, in a sign of the devastation wreaked by civil war.
item.dc:creator: Richard C. Paddock
